title: Is AI in trading really about prediction, or just better execution?
author: Few_Commercial_1313
selftext:
I’ve been trying to understand the real role of AI in trading systems, beyond the usual idea of “predicting the market.”

The more I look into it, the more it seems like AI isn’t necessarily about being right all the time.

It’s more about:

structuring decisions clearly  
executing without hesitation  
maintaining consistency across different conditions

In that sense, AI feels less like an edge on its own, and more like a tool that depends entirely on how well the underlying logic is built.

What I find interesting is that even advanced systems can struggle if they’re not designed to adapt to changing environments.

Curious how others here see it, do you view AI as a real edge, or just a more efficient way to execute existing strategies?

comments.author: EJCube
comments.body: I’m no expert but I absolutely view it as the latter—an edge that only exits if the underlying strategy is sound. I don’t trust AI to build its own strategies. It’s not trained on that. But I do trust it to act without pesky human emotion and also faster than any human can. That’s its use, for me

comments.author: UnitedAcanthaceae118
comments.body:
Both.

I use a prompt on Everstrike to trade only when the spread is narrow.

Helps cut down on execution costs.

comments.author: Spirited_Syllabub488
comments.body:
I have my own probability based prediction model developed with Neural Nets. My system is tested on 6+ years of BTC data and live trading for 6+ months now.

Sharpe of >3, CAGR of \~56% with a Max DD of \~25%. It has beaten BTC by 1,500% alpha. 

It has the ability to adapt with newer market data through retraining the system again on newer market data.

Would anyone like to see the report and live trades data?

I am looking to sell it due to capital drawback, I am unable to use this system at its potential.

comments.author: jabberw0ckee
comments.body:
\*More efficient way to execute existing strategies

I use AI for statistical analysis of my algorithmic alerts. First, the algo filters the highest performing stocks for 3 strategies and updates the lists every 2 weeks to keep the performance moving forward.

Then, the system tracks candles and alerts when the stocks are oversold. Dynamic data is saved for every alert: News, Price Targets, Analyst Ratings, RSI, Price, PEG Ratio, Volume - RVOL and PACE, Mean Reversion Distance, Price Target Distance, Time of Day - market time, Chart Formations, Support and Resistance Levels, and more.

The AI is a trade advisor who can perform statistical analysis on the already high win rate of the raw alerts which is 84%. After statistical analysis, the win rate reaches into the 90%+ range.

comments.author: OkLobster33
comments.body: You’re definitely on to something. A lot of people think AI is just a crystal ball for picking the next runner, but that’s where they usually get wrecked because it can’t account for everything like a major news drop or a black swan event.

comments.author: partha_33
comments.body:
the framing you're describing is pretty accurate. AI doesn't manufacture edge from nothing, it removes the execution noise that bleeds existing edge away. consistency, speed, no hesitation on a valid signal.

the limitation is that most systems still break when market regime shifts because the underlying logic was built for one environment. seeing how real traders adapt to that in practice is useful, markets.xyz surfaces that kind of thing.

comments.author: hydronos
comments.body:
Both, but the execution side is underrated and that's where most of the real value lives.



After 6+ months building a live system, the "prediction" framing is mostly a distraction. You can have a perfectly sound signal with 70%+ historical win rate and still lose money consistently if you act on it inconsistently, size wrong, or let hesitation creep in during volatility. AI handles all of that.



What I actually use AI for: a filter layer that scores each signal before it goes out. The screener finds the candidate, but the AI gate decides if the setup is clean enough given current market regime, IV environment, options flow context. That's not prediction, that's structured decision-making at a speed and consistency a human can't replicate manually across 150+ symbols every minute.



The regime shift problem is real though. The hardest part of building this has been making the system know when to slow down or step aside entirely rather than keep firing signals into a broken tape. That's not an AI problem, it's a strategy design problem. The AI just executes whatever logic you give it.



So: real edge comes from the strategy. AI keeps you from bleeding that edge away through sloppy execution and inconsistency.

comments.author: misscoffeeaddict2
comments.body: i agree with you. execution is huge, but having ai handle the "market intelligence" side is where the real time-saver is. i use [signalwhisper.com](http://signalwhisper.com) for this reason. it doesn't just guess; it structures the data and gives institutional-grade signals so i can focus on the execution part without the emotional hesitation you mentioned.

comments.author: MoreView2660
comments.body:
I've been testing AI a lot for trading. After spending a lot of time developing pine scripts and MT5 EA, I came to the conclusion that market can't be solved with algorithmic only. Well you can certainly a profitable algorithmic system... until market conditions change.

One of the feature I find fascinating with AI is the "Vision" capability. Based on that feature alone, I found that AI was capable of reasoning like a trader would do. Read the charts, apply a strategy, define zones and "predict" what the market will do.

If you add to that the structured reasoning an AI is capable of, you have a wonderful tool to really enhance your trading. Not only AI can help identify zones and levels, but it can also help with your money management, which should be the key for a profitable trader.

  
Based on that, I'm currently testing a new platform that you can use like TradingView, but with an AI assistant embedded. The model is completely different, and built on AI agentic concepts.  
Basically the AI assistant has direct access to the chart and technical indicators (OHLC, ICT zones, Market structure, EMA, RSI,...).  
Based on the trader request, the AI agent decides in full autonomy which information he needs : he can do multi-timeframes analysis, ICT, or any strategy you want him to use.  
When analysis is done, he can draw zones and levels directly on the charts, and can suggest setups with appropriate risk based on capital for money management. Setups are delivered in mini "card" format with an execute button.  
You can also connect the platform with MT5 and cTrader for direct trade execution.  
At the end, when you have the setup ready, and all the analysis, your only job is to "press the button" to confirm execution.

Honestly, I've been using it since a few weeks now, and results are really great.

If you want to test it, access is free. I'm actually looking for feedbacks on the platform.
